mnemonics.title: Perfect Matching Conditions
mnemonics.content: For perfect matching: EVEN vertices needed, all must be PAIRED. Remember: 'Perfect Means Every Vertex Gets A Partner' (EMVGP).

mnemonics.title: Hall's Theorem
mnemonics.content: Remember Hall's as 'Neighbors Must Numerically Exceed or Equal Subset Size' - N(S) ≥ |S| for all S⊆A.

mnemonics.title: König's Theorem
mnemonics.content: König connects K-atching (matching) to K-over (vertex cover) - both K-words mean maximum equals minimum.

acronyms.expansion: Neighborhood of S - set of all vertices adjacent to vertices in S
acronyms.usage: Used in Hall's Marriage Theorem to check matching conditions

acronyms.expansion: Matching - set of edges with no common vertices
acronyms.usage: Standard notation for any matching in graph theory

acronyms.expansion: Vertex set and Edge set of graph G
acronyms.usage: Used to denote the fundamental components of a graph
Part of Tutte's theorem for checking perfect matching existence

visualTips.visualization: Think of matching as pairing up dance partners - each person dances with at most one partner, no sharing allowed.
Imagine alternating colored edges (red for matched, blue for unmatched) forming a path from an unmatched start to an unmatched end.
Draw two columns - left side workers, right side jobs. Draw lines only from left to right, matching means each worker gets one job.
Place 'guards' on vertices such that every street (edge) is watched by at least one guard.

analogies.analogy: Matching workers to jobs is like finding dance partners - each worker can take one job, each job needs one worker.
Perfect matching is like pairing everyone at a dance - no one is left without a partner.
Augmenting path is like finding a way to improve your matching by swapping partners along a chain of changes.
Hall's condition is like checking if there are enough dance partners for every group of friends - each group of k friends needs at least k available partners.
